draft.title: a CRM for freelancers
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
a CRM for freelancers

Building your first draft…

facade.hypothesis: Freelancers respond better to friendly, conversational tone over corporate formality
facade.label: Casual and approachable
facade.acceptImplies: Voice should be warm, use contractions, acknowledge freelancer lifestyle challenges; build trust through personality not authority
facade.rejectImplies: Voice should be polished and formal; establish credibility through professional distance and structured language; assume users want business-like efficiency

facade.hypothesis: Freelancers prioritize a single metric (revenue, upcoming work, time to deadline) over detailed breakdowns, especially on mobile and in quick check-ins
facade.label: One big number
facade.acceptImplies: Build around glanceable single-stat dashboards; minimize tables and grids on primary views
facade.rejectImplies: Build comprehensive data views by default; show full context and multiple metrics simultaneously

evidence.hypothesis: Freelancers respond better to friendly, conversational tone over corporate formality
evidence.implication: Voice should be warm, use contractions, acknowledge freelancer lifestyle challenges; build trust through personality not authority

facade.hypothesis: Freelancers respond better to soft, approachable color palettes than stark white or dark interfaces
facade.axisTargeted: Light vs dark spectrum and overall warmth
facade.label: Warm cream background
facade.acceptImplies: Design should use warm neutrals, off-whites, and soft earth tones to create psychological safety and reduce interface fatigue
facade.rejectImplies: Design should lean toward crisp whites, cool grays, or dark modes to maintain professional clarity and visual hierarchy

facade.hypothesis: Freelancers prefer a persistent sidebar for navigation (clients, projects, invoices) paired with card-based layouts in the main area, reducing cognitive load from too many menu levels
facade.axisTargeted: Navigation architecture (sidebar vs tabs) combined with content grouping (cards vs lists)
facade.label: Sidebar with cards
facade.acceptImplies: Explore density within cards, test sidebar collapse behavior, probe whether cards should stack vertically or horizontally
facade.rejectImplies: Move toward tab-based or top-nav navigation; test list-based content instead of cards; consider whether a more unified single-column layout suits freelancer workflows better

facade.hypothesis: Freelance CRM users prefer immediate feedback on client data entry—forms validate and save without clicking a submit button, reducing friction in rapid client capture workflows.
facade.axisTargeted: Gesture vs click / Fluid vs snappy
facade.label: Instant form submit
facade.acceptImplies: Move toward auto-save patterns, real-time validation feedback, continuous data persistence as user types.
facade.rejectImplies: Move toward explicit confirmation (submit buttons, confirmation dialogs), bounded transaction steps, user-controlled checkpoint moments.

facade.hypothesis: For a CRM targeting freelancers, a warm, inviting interface (soft colors, rounded corners, gentle animations) will feel more human and less corporate than typical enterprise tools, making daily check-ins feel less sterile.
facade.label: Warm glow
facade.acceptImplies: User gravitates toward comfort and approachability; CRM should feel like a trusted ally, not a demanding boss. Soft palette, human typography, forgiving interactions.
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers clarity and efficiency over coziness; CRM should signal professionalism and precision. Likely cooler tones, sharp typography, minimal ornament.

axes.label: Whether user prefers minimal, clean aesthetics vs. rich visual hierarchy with color/imagery—reveals if they want a 'blank canvas' CRM or one with visual warmth and personality
Whether user wants tools clustered in one sidebar/dashboard vs. spread across tabs and modals—reveals navigation preference for a freelancer juggling multiple clients
Whether user responds to professional/formal tone vs. casual/encouraging tone—reveals if they want a business tool or a supportive coach
Whether user wants a calm, distraction-free workspace vs. energetic, motivational environment—reveals stress tolerance and work style for solo freelancers
Whether user prefers numbers/metrics front-and-center vs. narrative summaries and stories—reveals how they track success and make decisions
Whether user wants instant feedback and snappy interactions vs. smooth, deliberate transitions—reveals patience level and preference for responsiveness in a busy workflow

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers minimal, clean aesthetics vs. rich visual hierarchy with color/imagery—reveals if they want a 'blank canvas' CRM or one with visual warmth and personality
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Iris

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants tools clustered in one sidebar/dashboard vs. spread across tabs and modals—reveals navigation preference for a freelancer juggling multiple clients
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Prism

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user responds to professional/formal tone vs. casual/encouraging tone—reveals if they want a business tool or a supportive coach
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Lumen

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants a calm, distraction-free workspace vs. energetic, motivational environment—reveals stress tolerance and work style for solo freelancers
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Aura

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers numbers/metrics front-and-center vs. narrative summaries and stories—reveals how they track success and make decisions
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Facet

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants instant feedback and snappy interactions vs. smooth, deliberate transitions—reveals patience level and preference for responsiveness in a busy workflow
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Echo

facade.hypothesis: Freelancers prefer personality-driven interface copy (like Slack's tone) over generic SaaS—using humor, relatability, and self-aware language—because it builds psychological safety and reduces CRM friction
facade.axisTargeted: Voice personality spectrum: Quirky/relatable vs. Neutral/professional
facade.label: Slack-style friendly
facade.acceptImplies: Lean into casual, witty microcopy throughout the product (empty states, error messages, onboarding); consider brand voice guidelines that mirror freelancer communities (Discord, Twitter)
facade.rejectImplies: Keep voice businesslike and authoritative; prioritize clarity and formality over personality; treat copy as functional labels rather than relationship-building touchpoints
